Data Cleaning · EDA · SVR Forecasting · IEEE INCET 2023

Air Quality in Bangladesh: Cleaning, Exploration & COVID-19 Forecasting

End-to-end analysis of AQI readings across 10 Bangladeshi cities — from raw data cleaning and exploratory analysis through to Support Vector Regression forecasting of the COVID-19 lockdown's impact on air quality. Companion to IEEE INCET 2023.

PythonPandasData CleaningEDASVRForecasting

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

Air Quality Index Analysis in Bangladesh

A longitudinal study of pollution trends across 13 cities from 2014–2022, examining seasonal patterns, urban disparities, and the environmental impact of COVID-19 lockdowns.

📍 13 Cities 📅 2014 – 2022 📊 ~21,000 Records 🐍 Python · Pandas · Seaborn

📌 Background & Context¶

Bangladesh is consistently ranked among the most air-polluted countries in the world. Rapid urbanization, industrial growth, brick kilns, and vehicle emissions have made cities like Dhaka and Rangpur hotspots for particulate matter.

The Air Quality Index (AQI) is a standardized scale that communicates how polluted the air is and what health effects may be of concern. It is calculated from key pollutants including PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and O₃.

AQI Range Category Health Implication
0 – 50 🟢 Good Air quality is satisfactory
51 – 100 🟡 Moderate Acceptable; some pollutants may concern sensitive people
101 – 150 🟠 Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups Sensitive groups may experience effects
151 – 200 🔴 Unhealthy Everyone may begin to experience effects
201 – 300 🟣 Very Unhealthy Health alert: serious effects for everyone
301+ 🟤 Hazardous Emergency conditions; entire population affected

Dataset Source: Department of Environment (DoE), Bangladesh — daily AQI readings across major cities, collected via government monitoring stations.

3. Data Quality Assessment ¶

Before removing any rows, it's important to understand the pattern of missing data. Blind deletion can mask systematic sensor failures or data collection gaps.

Key issues found in the raw data:

  • AQI RANGE column is entirely empty — redundant, safe to drop
  • Air Quality Index (AQI) contains 'DNA' strings (interpreted as missing) and true NaNs
  • LOCATION contains 38 dirty variants of just 13 city names (e.g. 'DHAKAc', 'Dhakaa', 'DhakaC', 'gongb')
  • Dates are unsorted and stored as strings (dd-mm-yyyy)
⚠️  Total missing/invalid records : 5,287  (20.1% of raw data)

💡 Notable: Khulna (94% in 2014), Savar (100% in 2021) — likely sensor outages, not true absence of pollution.

4. Data Cleaning ¶

With the missing data pattern understood, we now apply a structured cleaning pipeline:

  1. Drop AQI RANGE — column is entirely empty
  2. Drop AQI CATEGORY — will be reconstructed from AQI values using WHO standard thresholds
  3. Standardise location names (38 dirty variants → 13 clean city names)
  4. Remove DNA and NaN records — confirmed as genuine sensor failures
  5. Parse and sort dates (dd-mm-yyyy strings → datetime)
  6. Convert AQI to numeric and remove invalid zeros
  7. Reconstruct AQI category using WHO standard brackets + standard colors
══════════════════════════════════════════════════
  CLEANING SUMMARY
══════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Raw records        :  26,251
  After cleaning     :  20,831  (79.4% retained)
  Records removed    :   5,420
  Cities             : 13
  Date range         : 2014-02-17  →  2022-06-08
  AQI mean           : 149.1
  AQI median         : 126.0
  AQI max            : 821
══════════════════════════════════════════════════

5. Exploratory Data Analysis ¶

5.1 Overview Statistics ¶

count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
AQI Statistics 20831.0 149.1 103.1 3.0 64.0 126.0 207.0 821.0

5.2 AQI Category Distribution ¶

How often does the air quality fall into each WHO category across all cities and years?

5.3 City Rankings ¶

Which cities are breathing the cleanest — and the most dangerous — air?

5.4 Seasonal Patterns ¶

Bangladesh's air quality is strongly seasonal. The dry winter months (October–March) trap pollutants near the surface, while the monsoon season (June–September) clears the air dramatically.

5.5 Year-over-Year Trends ¶

How has air quality evolved across the 9-year period? Is it getting better or worse?

5.6 Time Series & COVID-19 Context ¶

Bangladesh imposed strict lockdowns in April–June 2020 and April–June 2021. We examine whether these restrictions had any measurable effect on air quality in Dhaka, the capital.

A 30-day rolling average is applied to smooth daily noise and reveal the underlying trend.

Dhaka AQI — Pre-lockdown baseline (2019):     168.7
Dhaka AQI — Lockdown 1 (Apr–Jun 2020):        77.5  (-54.1% vs 2019)
Dhaka AQI — Lockdown 2 (Apr–Jun 2021):        165.5  (-1.9% vs 2019)
Dhaka AQI — 2022 average:                     229.6

5.7 Proportion of Unhealthy Air Days ¶

Mean AQI can be misleading — a city could have moderate average AQI but still have many dangerous spike days. Here we look at what fraction of days exceed each WHO threshold.

5.8 AQI Distribution by City ¶

Box plots reveal the full spread of each city's readings — including outliers and variance — beyond what the mean alone can show.

6. Key Findings ¶

🏭 1. Air Pollution is a National Crisis¶

43% of all readings across Bangladesh fall into the Unhealthy or worse category — this is not a niche concern for sensitive groups, it is a population-wide health emergency.

🌆 2. Rangpur is the Most Polluted City — Not Dhaka¶

With a mean AQI of 200.5 (Very Unhealthy), Rangpur consistently outranks Dhaka (171.3). Industrial activity, brick kilns, and limited green cover are likely drivers. Sylhet (avg 108) is the cleanest city by a wide margin.

❄️ 3. Seasonality is Extreme and Predictable¶

January's average AQI of 277 (Hazardous) is 5× higher than July's 52 (Good). The monsoon acts as a natural air filter. Any air quality intervention must be designed around this seasonal cycle.

🦠 4. COVID-19 Lockdowns Had a Limited but Visible Effect¶

Dhaka's AQI during the 2020 lockdown (Apr–Jun) was measurably lower than the same period in surrounding years. However, the effect was moderate — Bangladesh's AQI is overwhelmingly driven by seasonal and industrial factors rather than traffic alone.

📈 5. 2021 Was the Worst Year on Record¶

The national average in 2021 reached 201 AQI — the only year to cross into the Very Unhealthy threshold. This coincided with Bangladesh's severe COVID-19 second wave and warrants further investigation.

🔧 6. Sensor Reliability is a Major Data Quality Issue¶

Savar was 100% missing in 2021. Khulna had 94% missing data in 2014. Any conclusions about these cities must be interpreted with caution. Investment in sensor infrastructure is a prerequisite for evidence-based air quality policy.

Analysis by Mohammed Tahmid Hossain · saditahmid.github.io · Related publication: IEEE INCET 2023

Part 2 of 2

SVR Forecasting — Reproducing IEEE INCET 2023

Building on the cleaned dataset above: per-city AQI trends across pre/during/post-lockdown periods, and a Support Vector Regression model forecasting daily AQI (~6.2% MAPE in the original paper).

TIME-SERIES ANALYSIS & FORECASTING

COVID-19 Lockdowns & Air Quality in Bangladesh

Reproducing the analysis from IEEE INCET 2023: per-city AQI trends across three COVID-19 periods (pre / during / post lockdown), and daily AQI forecasting with Support Vector Regression.

🏙️ 10 Cities 📅 2014 – 2022 📈 SVR (RBF) 🐍 Python · scikit-learn

📌 About This Notebook¶

This notebook reproduces "Impact of COVID-19 Lockdowns on Air Quality in Bangladesh: Analysis and AQI Forecasting with Support Vector Regression" (IEEE INCET 2023). It has two parts :

  1. Location-wise analysis — per-city AQI (mean / min / max) over 2014–2022, with the three COVID-19 periods shaded: Before (2014–2019), During lockdown (2020), and After (2021).
  2. SVR forecasting — a Support Vector Regressor predicting daily-mean AQI, evaluated by MAPE.

The paper defines three periods precisely: Before COVID = 2014-02-17 → 2019-12-31, During Lockdown = 2020 (full year), After Lockdown = 2021.

⚠️ Note on reproducibility. The paper reports 6.2% MAPE. The exact preprocessed ("silver") dataset that produced that figure was lost. This notebook rebuilds everything from the surviving raw readings (case_data.csv). Spot-checking against the paper's Table II confirms the pipeline is faithful — e.g. 2014-09-02 gives a daily mean of exactly 46.5, matching the paper — but early-2015 daily means differ because the lost file averaged a different city subset per day. The reconstructed MAPE (~13–17%) is therefore higher than the original; the method, hyperparameters (RBF, γ=0.5, C=10, ε=0.05), and train/test split match the publication exactly.

1. Setup¶

✅ Setup complete.

2. Load & Clean Data¶

Following the paper's preprocessing: drop NaN dates/locations/AQI, replace DNA with NaN and drop, drop the insignificant AQI Range column, fix spelling errors in city names, and sort chronologically.

Clean readings : 20,831
Date range     : 2014-02-17 -> 2022-06-08
Cities (13)    : Barishal, Chittagong, Cumilla, Dhaka, Gazipur, Khulna, Mymensingh, Narayanganj, Narsingdi, Rajshahi, Rangpur, Savar, Sylhet

3. Location-Wise Analysis¶

The paper's core visual analysis (Figs 3–12): for each of the 10 major cities, plot monthly AQImean, AQImin, and AQImax over 2014–2022, with the three COVID-19 periods shaded. This reveals how each city's air quality shifted before, during, and after the 2020 lockdown.

3.1 Period Comparison — Mean AQI Before / During / After¶

Condensing the per-city story into one view: mean AQI in each COVID period per city. The paper's central finding is that most cities dipped during the 2020 lockdown, then rose sharply afterward.

Before (2014-19) During (2020) After (2021)
City
Barishal 134.1 151.4 175.4
Chittagong 123.1 93.9 132.3
Cumilla 106.1 118.1 160.7
Dhaka 164.3 151.7 250.5
Gazipur 160.7 163.1 237.5
Khulna 126.9 100.9 184.5
Mymensingh 134.4 189.9 237.9
Narsingdi 157.0 104.8 150.8
Rajshahi 135.1 118.8 206.2
Sylhet 102.1 107.4 149.4

4. SVR Forecasting¶

Now the forecasting component (paper Section V-A). Following the paper exactly:

  • Resample to a daily-mean AQI series
  • Train: 2014-02-28 → 2020-12-31 · Test: 2021-01-01 → 2022-06-01
  • Scale to (0,1) with MinMaxScaler
  • SVR: RBF kernel, gamma=0.5, C=10, epsilon=0.05
Daily-mean series: 2,735 days
Train: 2,429 days  |  Test: 291 days

Test MAPE : 12.73%   (paper: ~6.2% on the lost preprocessed set)
Test MAE  : 21.26 AQI points
Test R²   : 0.837

4.1 Actual vs Predicted — Full Timeline¶

Reproducing the paper's Fig. 2: actual (red) vs predicted (blue) AQI across the entire 2014–2022 span. Predictions on the training portion track closely; the test portion (2021+) shows genuine forecasting.

4.2 Actual vs Predicted Sample (paper Table II)¶

A sample of actual vs predicted values, matching the paper's Table II format.

Actual AQI Predicted AQI
Date
2014-02-20 267.143 208.621
2014-11-01 217.500 143.063
2015-06-16 61.167 57.939
2016-02-07 401.167 313.257
2016-09-23 54.000 54.179
2017-05-11 88.714 119.295
2017-12-24 267.250 243.517
2018-08-08 74.250 57.176
2019-03-23 153.125 108.280
2019-11-11 63.375 57.604
2020-07-10 33.333 48.985
2021-03-11 140.364 123.481

5. Key Findings¶

🏙️ 1. Air Pollution is Severe Nationwide¶

Across all 10 cities, AQI is alarmingly high — Dhaka, Chittagong, and Gazipur (industrial/commercial hubs) are the worst. The winter months (Dec–Mar, the dry season) consistently show the highest AQI due to low rainfall.

🦠 2. Lockdown Lowered AQI — But Only Modestly¶

Most cities show a visible dip in the shaded 2020 lockdown window. However, the national 2020 average was only barely lower than 2019 — the paper attributes this to inconsistent lockdown enforcement across the country.

📈 3. Sharp Post-Lockdown Rebound¶

The After (2021) bars are the tallest for most cities — Dhaka jumped to ~250, Gazipur to ~230 — as mills, factories, and traffic returned to full activity.

📉 4. SVR Captures the Trend Well¶

The Support Vector Regressor tracks the AQI signal closely across the full timeline, reproducing the paper's Fig. 2. Reconstructed MAPE lands higher than the paper's 6.2% because the original preprocessed dataset was lost — but the method, hyperparameters, and evaluation protocol match the publication, and spot-checks against Table II confirm the pipeline is faithful.

Analysis by Mohammed Tahmid Hossain · saditahmid.github.io · Published: IEEE INCET 2023
